The Hall of Literary Brilliance smelled of sandalwood and old ink. The emperor sat on his dragon throne, his face gaunt under the weight of empire, his eyes hollow. Meizhu stood before him, qin in hand, veil lifted just enough to let the light catch her gaze. The court rustled behind her like autumn leaves—scholars, censors, attendants whose names she could not recall and whose loyalty she no longer needed to guess.

She lifted the instrument.

The first note shivered into the air, thin as a spider’s thread. It trembled, then settled into a melody no one had written—only she could hear it, only she could shape it. The emperor’s fingers drummed against the armrest. Zhao Gou stood to the side, motionless as a painted figure, watching not the brushwork of her technique but the way her shadow flickered against the silk banner behind her.

A censor leaned forward. “Your Majesty, she plays the *Spring on the Moonlit Terrace*—a classic of harmony.”

Meizhu’s thumb brushed the strings. The melody shifted. The censor frowned.

Another joined. “It’s the standard healing mode. For the emperor’s humors.”

A third nodded eagerly. “Her touch is legendary.”

Zhao Gou said nothing. His eyes tracked the qin’s curve, the way Meizhu’s wrist turned—not rigid, not rehearsed, but alive. The emperor exhaled, a sound like wind through a cracked window.

Meizhu played.

The tune deepened. The air thickened. She wove in the rhythm of rain on a roof, the creak of a loom at dawn, the whispered exhale of a scribe dipping his brush. She played the Jia family ledger—numbers, dates, embezzled bushels of rice turned into gold and forgiven debts. She played the forged edicts, the ink still wet on Jia’s blade. She played the hollow thud of a seal stamped with Lin blood, the shiver of a widow’s sleeve as she stood over a son’s grave.

The court swayed.

A scholar gasped. “She’s altering the tempo.”

Another clutched his sleeve. “The notes are… they’re rearranging.”

Meizhu’s fingers moved faster. The emperor’s breathing slowed. His eyelids grew heavy. The court began to sway—not from the music, but from the weight of what they did not understand.

Zhao Gou’s voice cut through the hum. “She’s not healing you, Your Majesty. She’s showing you what you allowed.”

The emperor’s lips parted. No sound came.

Meizhu played a rising arpeggio. The chandelier above trembled. Dust spiraled like gray ash.

Then, at the crescendo, she paused. Her fingers hovered over the strings. The silence that followed was thicker than incense smoke.

The emperor blinked.

Meizhu spoke, voice low, clear.

“The debt is paid.”

The court erupted. Censors shouted. Attendants bowed. A man in crimson robes stumbled back, knocking over a scroll holder. Yellow paper scattered across marble like dying leaves.

Zhao Gou didn’t move. He watched her. Watched the way her chest rose once, slow and deep, beneath the silk of her gown. Watched the way her fingers curled into a fist at her side, just for a breath, before she lifted the qin again.

The emperor’s voice was a whisper.

“What debt?”

Meizhu did not answer. She began to play again—this time softer, slower. A lullaby. A ghost song.

The emperor’s head dipped forward. His crown dipped with it, teetering. Attendants gasped. Zhao Gou stepped forward—only an inch—but Meizhu’s hand lifted, palm out, still.

The emperor slept.

The melody rolled on, a ribbon of sound unspooling through the hall. Meizhu played the names of the dead—her father, the farmers who starved, the soldiers who bled for Jia’s lies. She played the Jia family crest burning in a brazier. She played the year her name was erased from the records.

The court watched in stunned silence.

A censor in the front row swallowed hard. He reached for his brush, then stopped. He had no scroll to record this.

Zhao Gou’s voice cut through the drone.

“Physician Lin.”

Meizhu’s fingers stilled for half a breath.

“You have done what none dared.”

She lowered the qin. Looked at him.

“Not I,” she said. “The strings did.”

He stepped closer. Close enough that the silk of his sleeve brushed the hem of her gown. The court’s murmurs faded into the background, irrelevant.

“You gave the emperor back his sight,” Zhao Gou said. “He will remember this.”

Meizhu lifted the veil over her face. “He’ll remember a dream. That’s all mercy is.”

Zhao Gou’s jaw tightened. “You don’t believe in mercy.”

“I believe in debt.” She turned toward the throne. “And he owed me one.”

The emperor stirred. His crown righted. His eyes fluttered open.

Jia Sidao’s empty seat gleamed at the far end of the hall.

Zhao Gou exhaled through his nose. “You invited the ghosts to dinner.”

Meizhu walked toward the throne. “Ghosts keep better company than living men.”

The emperor’s gaze followed her. He did not speak. He only sipped the tea a servant offered, hands trembling.

Zhao Gou moved beside her. “He will ask for your name.”

Meizhu kept walking. “He already knows it.”

“He will want to thank you.”

“Let him thank the qin.”

They reached the foot of the dais. Meizhu set the instrument down. The final note hummed in the air like a struck bell waiting to fade.

The emperor looked at her.

Then he spoke.

“You.”

The word was not a question. It was a verdict.

Zhao Gou’s hand twitched at his side.

Meizhu bowed. Deep. The way a subject honors a wound.

The emperor’s voice was raw.

“What is your name?”

She rose.

“Lin Meizhu,” she said.

The emperor’s eyes widened.

Zhao Gou went very still.

The court fell silent.

The emperor’s cup slipped from his fingers. It shattered on the marble floor, tea staining the cracks like blood.
